items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Lebanon: Flash Update #39 - Escalation of hostilities in Lebanon (as of 29 June 2026)
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Lebanon
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached file.

HIGHLIGHTS

  • At least 4,247 deaths and 12,195 injuries due to hostilities recorded by the Ministry of Public Health since 2 March.
  • A total of 52,243 Internally Displaced Persons (over 52 per cent women and girls) remained in 479 collective shelters as of 29 June.
  • Since 2 March, 49,328 children under five, adolescent girls, and pregnant and breastfeeding women have been reached with emergency nutrition rations and micronutrient supplements.
  • The 2026 Lebanon Flash Appeal is 37 per cent funded, with approximately US$237million received against US$639.9 million required.

SITUATION OVERVIEW

The humanitarian situation in Lebanon remains critical, with protection risks and significant gaps in essential services persisting. Following the latest ceasefire announcement, military activity in Lebanon has decreased. However, daily airstrikes, shelling, demolitions, and nationwide military drone activity continued to be reported.

Returns have increased, particularly in South and Nabatieh governorates, yet remained limited as the security situation remains fragile and destruction of housing and infrastructure hinder safe return. Some displaced families are relocating to shelters closer to their areas of origin to conduct “go-and-see” visits to assess conditions, including levels of damage and safety. Priority needs for returning populations include shelter support, restoration of basic services, and unexploded ordnance (UXO) clearance.

As of 29 June, 704,445 people remain internally displaced, a 17 per cent decrease from 17 June, with 91 per cent of IDPs coming from just five districts: Nabatieh, Bent Jbeil, Marjaayoun, Sour, and Baabda. Approximately 479 collective shelters remain operational as of 28 June, hosting 52, 243 people. This reflects a decrease of 7,500 individuals in shelters and the closure of 46 sites since 25 June according to the Lebanese Red Cross and National Disaster Risk Management Unit. For families that remain displaced, prolonged and repeated displacement are eroding coping mechanisms and further heightening vulnerability.

The Ministry of Public Health reported 4,247 people killed (including 390 Women and 253 children) and 12,195 injured (1449 women and 1036 children) as of 28 June. 135 health care professionals have been killed on duty and 406 reported as injured since 2 March.

Funding constraints remain a major challenge to sustaining humanitarian assistance. As of 29 June, the 2026Lebanon Flash Appeal has received US$ 237 million, or 37 per cent of the $639.9 million requested.

The humanitarian situation in Lebanon remains critical, with protection risks and significant gaps in essential services persisting. Following the latest ceasefire announcement, military activity in Lebanon has decreased. However, daily airstrikes, shelling, demolitions, and nationwide military drone activity continued to be reported.
Returns have increased, particularly in South and Nabatieh governorates, yet remained limited as the security situation remains fragile and destruction of housing and infrastructure hinder safe return. Some displaced families are relocating to shelters closer to their areas of origin to conduct “go-and-see” visits to assess conditions, including levels of damage and safety. Priority needs for returning populations include shelter support, restoration of basic services, and unexploded ordnance (UXO) clearance.
As of 29 June, 704,445 people remain internally displaced, a 17 per cent decrease from 17 June, with 91 per cent of IDPs coming from just five districts: Nabatieh, Bent Jbeil, Marjaayoun, Sour, and Baabda. Approximately 479 collective shelters remain operational as of 28 June, hosting 52, 243 people. This reflects a decrease of 7,500 individuals in shelters and the closure of 46 sites since 25 June according to the Lebanese Red Cross and National Disaster Risk Management Unit. For families that remain displaced, prolonged and repeated displacement are eroding coping mechanisms and further heightening vulnerability.
The Ministry of Public Health reported 4,247 people killed (including 390 Women and 253 children) and 12,195 injured (1449 women and 1036 children) as of 28 June. 135 health care professionals have been killed on duty and 406 reported as injured since 2 March.
Funding constraints remain a major challenge to sustaining humanitarian assistance. As of 29 June, the 2026Lebanon Flash Appeal has received US$ 237 million, or 37 per cent of the $639.9 million requested.

Country: Nigeria
Source: Fact Foundation

KEY FINDINGS

  • The Minimum Expenditure Basket (MEB)* declined by 4% to NGN 193,485 (USD 140.76) in the reporting month, indicating a modest easing of financial pressure on the purchasing power of affected households.
  • Staple cereals and legumes showed a declined availability; however Oil and animal protein increases in availability.
  • Energy items: Fuel commodities availability droped with petrol reported -6%, diesel down by -3% and charcoal down by -7%. While Water widely availability at (73%) across all markets.
  • Cash (Naira) remains the dominant payment mode at 48%. Digital payment penetration remains limited despite some use of bank transfers (24%) and debit cards (12%).
  • 43% of vendors cited lack of capital as the primary reason for stock shortages; 18% cited loss of supplier credit.
